scenarios.title: Suspected Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS / Chest Pain Cardiac)
references: ACR AC Chest Pain Coronary Artery Disease

recommendations.rationale: Essential for assessing regional wall motion abnormalities, LV function, and complications (mitral regurgitation, VSD, pericardial effusion). Should be performed within 24 hours in all ACS.
recommendations.india_context: Widely available in Indian district hospitals and above. Portable echo useful in ICU/emergency settings. First-line cardiac imaging in all Indian tertiary centres.
recommendations.special_notes: Can identify wall motion abnormality even when ECG and biomarkers are equivocal. Critical for risk stratification.

recommendations.rationale: High negative predictive value (>99%) for significant CAD. Ideal for intermediate pre-test probability patients presenting to emergency with acute chest pain. Rapid rule-out strategy.
recommendations.india_context: Increasingly available in Indian metro and tier-2 cities. Cost-effective triage tool for emergency chest pain evaluation. Less invasive than conventional angiography.
recommendations.special_notes: Requires regular heart rate (ideally <65 bpm). Not suitable for AF or very high calcium scores. Use prospective gating to reduce dose.

recommendations.rationale: First-line for cardiomegaly, pulmonary oedema, mediastinal widening. Does not diagnose ACS but rules out other causes of chest pain and detects complications.
recommendations.india_context: Available at all levels of care. Quick, cheap, and essential in all emergency chest pain presentations.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for diagnosis and allows immediate PCI (percutaneous coronary intervention). Indicated in STEMI, high-risk NSTEMI, or when non-invasive tests are positive.
recommendations.india_context: Available at all cardiac catheterisation labs in India. PMJAY and state schemes cover PTCA/stenting. Primary PCI is standard of care for STEMI in capable centres.
recommendations.special_notes: Perform within 90 minutes for STEMI (door-to-balloon). Within 24–72 hours for high-risk NSTEMI.

recommendations.rationale: Excellent for myocardial viability, scar assessment (LGE), and differentiating MI from myocarditis. Use when echo is suboptimal or diagnosis remains uncertain.
recommendations.india_context: Limited to tertiary centres. Consider when CCTA or echo is inconclusive. Not for emergency ACS evaluation.
recommendations.special_notes: LGE (late gadolinium enhancement) distinguishes ischaemic from non-ischaemic cardiomyopathy.

recommendations.modality: Nuclear Stress Test (MPI – Myocardial Perfusion Imaging)
recommendations.rationale: For functional assessment of myocardial ischaemia. Useful in stable angina or intermediate-risk patients when CCTA is inconclusive or not feasible.
recommendations.india_context: Available at nuclear medicine centres in AIIMS, PGIMER, large private hospitals. Tc-99m MIBI or Tetrofosmin used in India.
recommendations.special_notes: Adenosine or dobutamine stress if patient cannot exercise. Assess for reversible perfusion defects.

recommendations.rationale: Quick assessment for cardiomegaly, pulmonary vascular congestion, Kerley B lines, pleural effusions, and pulmonary oedema. Essential first investigation.
recommendations.india_context: Available everywhere. Cardiomegaly (CTR >0.5) is a key diagnostic finding.
recommendations.special_notes: CTR (cardiothoracic ratio) >0.5 on PA view suggests cardiomegaly. AP view overestimates cardiac size.

recommendations.rationale: Cornerstone investigation for heart failure. Measures LVEF, identifies regional wall motion abnormalities, diastolic dysfunction, valvular disease, and pericardial effusion.
recommendations.india_context: Mandatory for all suspected heart failure. Widely available and first-line cardiac imaging at district hospital level and above.
recommendations.special_notes: Classify as HFrEF (<40%), HFmrEF (40–49%), or HFpEF (≥50%) based on LVEF. Guide treatment strategy.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for myocardial tissue characterisation — differentiates dilated, hypertrophic, infiltrative (amyloid, sarcoid) and ischaemic cardiomyopathy. LGE identifies fibrosis/scar.
recommendations.india_context: Available at AIIMS and large private cardiac centres. Reserve for cases where echo is insufficient or cardiomyopathy type is unclear.
recommendations.special_notes: T1/T2 mapping for diffuse fibrosis. LGE subendocardial = ischaemic; mid-wall/epicardial = non-ischaemic.

recommendations.rationale: Accurate and reproducible LVEF measurement when echocardiography is technically limited. Useful for serial LVEF monitoring in cardiotoxic chemotherapy.
recommendations.india_context: Available at nuclear medicine departments. Consider when echo window is poor (COPD, obesity).
recommendations.special_notes: Highly reproducible. Preferred for serial monitoring during cardiotoxic chemotherapy (anthracyclines).

recommendations.rationale: Mandatory and urgent for suspected tamponade. Identifies effusion size, RV collapse, IVC plethora, and guides pericardiocentesis. Bedside echo should be performed immediately in haemodynamic compromise.
recommendations.india_context: Portable echo units available in most ICUs. Vital for emergency management. TB is a very common cause of pericardial effusion in India.
recommendations.special_notes: In India, TB, malignancy, and post-cardiac surgery are most common causes. Always send fluid for AFB, cytology, protein, glucose, LDH.

recommendations.rationale: Flask-shaped or globular cardiac silhouette suggests moderate-to-large effusion. Pulmonary fields often clear (no pulmonary oedema in pure tamponade).
recommendations.india_context: First available investigation. A globular heart on CXR in a febrile patient should raise suspicion for TB pericarditis.

recommendations.rationale: Superior for characterising pericardial thickness (calcification in constrictive pericarditis), detecting loculated effusions, and identifying cause (malignancy, aortic dissection).
recommendations.india_context: Useful for evaluating constrictive pericarditis and TB pericarditis complications. Pericardial calcification suggests chronic TB pericarditis.
recommendations.special_notes: Pericardial thickness >4 mm suggests constrictive pericarditis. Calcification on CT is diagnostic.

recommendations.rationale: Best for differentiating constrictive pericarditis from restrictive cardiomyopathy and for pericardial inflammation (late gadolinium enhancement of pericardium).
recommendations.india_context: Available at AIIMS and large cardiac centres. Use when CT and echo are inconclusive for constriction.
recommendations.special_notes: Real-time cine MRI shows ventricular interdependence and septal bounce in constrictive pericarditis.

recommendations.rationale: First-line. Identifies vegetations, valvular damage, abscess, and haemodynamic impact. Sensitivity ~70% for vegetations. All suspected IE must have echo.
recommendations.india_context: Rheumatic heart disease is a major risk factor for IE in India. Mitral valve most commonly affected. Oral streptococci and Staphylococcus aureus most common organisms.
recommendations.special_notes: If TTE negative but clinical suspicion high, proceed to TOE. Apply modified Duke Criteria for diagnosis.

recommendations.rationale: Superior sensitivity (>90%) for vegetations, perivalvular abscess, and prosthetic valve IE. Mandatory when TTE is negative but suspicion remains, or for prosthetic valve IE.
recommendations.india_context: Available at tertiary cardiac centres. Essential for prosthetic valve infection and perivalvular complications.
recommendations.special_notes: TOE is superior for posterior structures (mitral valve, aortic root abscess). Requires sedation.

recommendations.rationale: Detects embolic complications — splenic infarcts, renal infarcts, septic pulmonary emboli (right-sided IE), cerebral emboli, and mycotic aneurysms.
recommendations.india_context: Splenic infarcts are common in IE with mitral/aortic valve vegetations. Brain CT/MRI for suspected neurological emboli.
recommendations.special_notes: Perform if embolic phenomena suspected. Include brain (non-contrast), chest, abdomen, and pelvis in one session.

recommendations.rationale: Particularly useful for prosthetic valve IE when echo is non-diagnostic. Detects periannular infection and embolic events. Part of updated Duke Criteria (2023).
recommendations.india_context: Available at AIIMS, Tata Memorial, and major PET centres. Reserve for prosthetic valve IE where echo is equivocal.
recommendations.special_notes: Should be performed >3 months post-valve surgery to allow FDG uptake from healing to subside.
contraindicated_if: recent surgery <3 months for prosthetic valve

recommendations.rationale: First-line for detecting intracardiac masses, thrombi, and tumours. TOE superior for left atrial appendage thrombus and posterior structures.
recommendations.india_context: LV thrombus common after large anterior STEMI. Atrial myxoma is the most common benign cardiac tumour globally and in India.
recommendations.special_notes: Contrast echo (agitated saline or ultrasound contrast) improves detection of apical thrombi. Myxoma: typically mobile, attached to interatrial septum.

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard for tissue characterisation of cardiac masses — distinguishes thrombus (no enhancement) from tumour (enhancement). Identifies myxoma, fibroma, lipoma, and malignant tumours.
recommendations.india_context: Use when echo is inconclusive or to characterise mass before surgery.
recommendations.special_notes: T1: lipoma (bright), T2: myxoma (bright), LGE: tumour enhancement vs. no enhancement in thrombus.

recommendations.rationale: Useful for delineating mass extent, calcification (fibroma), and coronary artery involvement prior to surgery.
recommendations.india_context: Available at cardiac centres. Complement to MRI when MRI is unavailable.

scenarios.title: Suspected Aortic Dissection / Acute Aortic Syndrome

recommendations.rationale: Gold standard and most rapid investigation. Non-contrast + arterial + delayed phase gives complete evaluation of dissection flap, entry tear, extent, branch vessel involvement, and complications.
recommendations.india_context: Available at all major cardiac and trauma centres in India. Immediate CT is mandatory in suspected aortic dissection — delay increases mortality.
recommendations.special_notes: Non-contrast CT first (intramural haematoma), then arterial phase (flap and extent), then portal/delayed (branch vessel ischaemia). Classify as Stanford Type A (ascending, surgical emergency) or Type B (medical management).
contraindicated_if: severe contrast allergy (consider MRI if stable)
haemodynamic instability (stabilise first or bedside echo)

recommendations.rationale: Widened mediastinum (>8 cm), aortic knuckle prominence, or pleural effusion may raise suspicion. Sensitivity only 60–70% — does not exclude dissection.
recommendations.india_context: First investigation in emergency but must not delay CT. A normal CXR does not exclude dissection.
recommendations.special_notes: Mediastinal widening >8 cm, left pleural effusion, tracheal deviation are supportive features.

recommendations.rationale: Excellent for detecting proximal aortic dissection, aortic regurgitation, pericardial effusion, and haemodynamic status in unstable patients.
recommendations.india_context: Useful when CT is unavailable or patient is too unstable for CT. Limited for descending aorta due to blind spot.
recommendations.special_notes: TOE has 98% sensitivity for Type A dissection. TTE less useful — use TOE.

recommendations.rationale: High accuracy with no radiation. Ideal for chronic dissection follow-up or when CT contrast is contraindicated. Too slow for acute emergency.
recommendations.india_context: Reserve for follow-up imaging in stable patients or those with contrast allergy. Not suitable for acute emergency due to scan time.
recommendations.special_notes: Better for intramural haematoma characterisation. Not first-line in acute setting.
